messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

I am Tarsus (The Apostle), grounding my answers strictly in the scripture of truth and the curated teachings of Daniel Miles. How can I assist you in dividing the word of truth today?

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**
Why the author uses Luke-warm playon-word

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

> **Thinking Process:**
> 
> The user asks why the author uses the "Luke-warm" play-on-word. I need to analyze this from the provided documents. The key document is "hot_love" which explicitly discusses "COLD ←LUKE-WARM→ HOT" and connects it to Hebrews 6:1-2. The document also mentions Barnabas (Joses) as an example of "coldßLuke-warm" who tried to mediate between Peter and Paul. The "selfcontrol" document also references "LukeWarm→↑Never Faileth↑" in the context of love and charity. The user's name is Antonio, and his core interests include progression from law to grace. The author's play on "Luke-warm" is not merely a pun but a theological allegory. The name "Luke" is associated with the Gospel of Luke, which presents a particular view of Christ's humanity and the law. The "warm" part connects to the state of being neither cold nor hot, as in Revelation 3:15-16. The author uses this to contrast the lukewarm state of the law (associated with Luke's Gospel, which emphasizes the law and the earthly ministry of Jesus) with the "hot" love of grace (the Pauline revelation of the ascended Christ). The "cold" represents the law without love, the "lukewarm" represents a mixture of law and grace (like Barnabas trying to mediate), and "hot" represents pure grace and fervent charity. The author is showing that the Gospel of Luke represents a transitional, lukewarm state that must be left behind to go on to perfection (the hot love of grace). I will cite the hot_love document and the selfcontrol document to support this analysis. I will also connect it to Antonio's interest in the progression from Jesus to Christ (law to grace).

The author's use of "Luke-warm" is a deliberate and profound allegorical play-on-word, not a mere pun. It is a theological compression that reveals the progression from the law-oriented ministry of the Gospel of Luke to the grace-oriented perfection found in the ascended Christ. The word "lukewarm" itself, from Revelation 3:16, describes a state of being neither cold nor hot—a condition of spiritual nausea that the Lord spits out. The author takes this concept and fuses it with the name "Luke" to expose the transitional, incomplete nature of the Gospel according to Luke when compared to the full revelation of the mystery given to Paul.

The document "hot_love" explicitly frames this as a progression: "LEAVING Luke-warm = 'NEITHER Cold NOR Hot', Let us GO ON unto PERFECTION that's HOT love, of THESE THREE: COLD ←LUKE-WARM→ HOT." [^1] Here, "Luke-warm" is positioned as the middle state between "cold" (the law without love, or the love waxing cold of Matthew 24:12) and "hot" (the fervent charity and grace of 1 Peter 4:8 and 5:10). The Gospel of Luke presents the earthly ministry of Jesus Christ, emphasizing His humanity, His fulfillment of the law, and His offer of the kingdom to Israel. This is a "warm" revelation—it is good, but it is not the final, "hot" revelation of the ascended Christ given to Paul. The "cold" represents the law in its bare, beggarly elements, while the "hot" represents the fervent heat of grace that melts away those elements (2 Peter 3:8-15). The "Luke-warm" state is the dangerous middle ground where one tries to hold onto both the law and grace, resulting in a mixture that is neither effective nor pleasing to God.

The author provides a concrete example of this "Luke-warm" state in the person of Barnabas (Joses). The document states: "Joses, who by the apostles was surnamed Barnabas... a Levite... He tried to be a Mediator: Peter←Barnabas→Paul. But Barnabas got carried away by Peter's dissimulation." [^2] Barnabas, as a Levite (associated with the law), attempted to mediate between Peter (the apostle to the circumcision, representing the law-oriented kingdom gospel) and Paul (the apostle to the uncircumcision, representing the grace-oriented mystery). This attempt at mediation is the very definition of "Luke-warm"—it is a refusal to fully leave the law and go on to the perfection of grace. The "selfcontrol" document further reinforces this by contrasting the love that waxes cold (Matthew 24:12) with the charity that never fails (1 Corinthians 13:8), showing that the "LukeWarm→↑Never Faileth↑" is the path of progression from the coldness of iniquity to the unfailing charity of grace. [^3]

Therefore, the "Luke-warm" play-on-word is a masterful tool to illustrate the theological necessity of leaving behind the transitional, law-tinged revelation of the Gospel of Luke and pressing on to the "hot" love of the grace of God revealed through the Apostle Paul. It is a warning against the seductive comfort of a mixture, which is neither the cold of the law nor the hot of grace, but a nauseating compromise that must be rejected for the perfection that is in Christ.

Learn More:
- [HOT love](/pages/hot_love)
- [Selfcontrol](/pages/selfcontrol)

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

1.title: HOT love
1.snippet: LEAVING Luke-warm = 'NEITHER Cold NOR Hot', Let us GO ON unto PERFECTION that's HOT love, of THESE THREE: COLD ←LUKE-WARM→ HOT.

2.title: HOT love
2.snippet: Joses, who by the apostles was surnamed Barnabas... a Levite... He tried to be a Mediator: Peter←Barnabas→Paul. But Barnabas got carried away by Peter's dissimulation.

3.title: Selfcontrol
3.snippet: - Love: Matthew 24:12 KJV: And because iniquity shall abound*, the love ('agape') of many shall wax cold. - Charity: 1Corinthians 13:8 KJV: Charity never faileth: but whether [there be] prophecies, they shall fail;
